build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: The player presses a key to charge a cast meter, then releases to throw the fishing line. Cast distance depends on timing. The line lands with a visible splash particle or ripple effect. Score 0 if there is no cast meter, if line placement is random with no player control, or if there is no splash effect on landing.

After casting, a bobber floats on the water. A fish bites with a visible tug animation. The player must press a key at the right moment to hook the fish, then manage line tension by alternately reeling and releasing. A tension meter shows current line stress. Score 0 if fish bite instantly with no wait, if there is no timing-based hook mechanic, or if the tension meter is missing.

Weather changes (sunny, rainy, foggy) affect fish behaviour: some species only appear in rain, others at dawn or dusk. A day/night cycle shifts lighting and water colour over time. Score 0 if weather never changes, if time of day is fixed, or if weather has no gameplay effect on fish availability.

At least five distinct fish species with different appearances, sizes, and habitats. A caught-fish screen shows the species name, size, and short flavour text. Rare fish sparkle or have unique colours. Score 0 if there are fewer than 5 species, if caught fish are not displayed with details, or if all fish look identical.

The world has at least three visually distinct water bodies: a calm lake (light blue, small fish), a rushing river (current effects, medium fish), and a deep ocean (dark blue, large rare fish). The player can walk freely on land and approach any shore. Score 0 if there are fewer than 3 water bodies, if shores are invisible, or if the player cannot move freely between them.

A fishing journal or bestiary tracks all caught species with a silhouette outline for uncaught fish. The player can see total species caught and a completion percentage. The journal updates immediately after catching a new fish. Score 0 if there is no journal, if uncaught fish are invisible in the list, or if the journal does not update after a catch.

Fish in different water bodies and weather conditions have noticeably different difficulty: ocean fish fight harder and require more tension management; river fish pull with the current; lake fish are easier catches. The player can perceive a progression in challenge across locations. Score 0 if all fish behave identically regardless of location, or if difficulty does not vary.

The full fishing loop is connected: Walk to shore, charge cast, cast line, wait for bite, hook at right moment, reel with tension management, catch fish, view in journal. Each step produces visible state changes. Score 0 if any step in the chain is missing or broken.

The full game loop is connected: Title screen, explore world, find water body, cast, catch fish, view journal, repeat. The player can experience every node without restarting the application. Score 0 if any transition in the chain is missing or broken.

A title screen is present and styled: it shows the game name in a custom or themed font, has at minimum a play button that is interactive and transitions to the game world, and uses a non-default background (water scene, sunset, or illustrated landscape) — not plain Godot grey or a solid-colour fill. Score 0 if the game launches directly into gameplay with no title screen, or if the start button is non-functional.

Overall art-style coherence: every visible element — water, land, fish, player character, UI — belongs to a single consistent visual language. The palette is serene and natural; no clashing neon or unrelated photorealistic assets. Score 0 if the screen mixes incompatible styles, or if elements are obviously unthemed placeholder shapes.

Environment, character, and UI art quality: water bodies use textured or illustrated surfaces with visible ripple detail; the player character is a recognisable sprite (not a plain coloured rectangle); fish have distinct sprites per species; HUD elements use styled, themed widgets — not raw unstyled Godot defaults. Score 0 if water is a uniform colour fill, if the player is a solid-colour primitive, or if primary UI elements are obviously unstyled default widgets.

Animation continuity and polish: cast charge meter, line arc, splash, bobber bobbing, fish tug, reel tension gauge, caught-fish screen, and day/night transitions each play as a smooth multi-frame tween or sprite animation — no element snaps to its new state in a single frame. Scene transitions use a brief fade rather than an instant cut. Score 0 if any of these moments is instantaneous, or if transitions are hard cuts with no tween.
